key_assumptions.input: Starting free cash flow per share
key_assumptions.source: FY2025 diluted EPS $6.66 less ~4% normalization haircut = $6.4/sh; regulated utility uses earnings power not OCF $6.9B less investing $11.9B
Rate-base CAGR from $72B capital plan; dividend payout ~56% on normalized earnings

open_questions: Confirm whether FY2025 EPS step-up is durable or partly weather/regulatory catch-up.
$5.6B equity issuance plan: timing and dilution impact on per-share earnings path.
Allowed ROE trajectory in Ohio, Texas, and West Virginia jurisdictions.
No approved third-party sources; re-scan when Substacks or fund letters added.
thesis: American Electric Power is one of the largest regulated electric utilities in the United States, serving customers across **11 states** through vertically integrated generation-and-distribution companies and standalone transmission-and-distribution utilities. **[Fact]** FY2025 operating revenues were **$21.9 billion** (+15% versus prior year). **[Fact]** Operating income was **$5.3 billion** and net income available to common shareholders was **$3.6 billion**, or **$6.66** diluted earnings per share. **[Fact]** The company is executing a **$72 billion** capital plan from 2026 through 2030 focused on transmission infrastructure, distribution upgrades, and new generation to serve rising load including data centers. **[Fact]** Consolidated debt carrying amount was **$47.7 billion** against **$197 million** of cash at December 31, 2025. **[Inference]** AEP earns allowed returns set by state regulators and FERC on invested capital (rate base), not through open-market pricing.
why_market_wrong: Utility investors often treat AEP as a bond proxy: safe dividend, modest growth, fairly priced for the sector. **[Inference]** That framing may **underweight the rate-base growth engine** in the $72 billion capital plan and large-load interconnection pipeline, which can compound earnings faster than the current yield alone implies. **[Inference]** Conversely, the market may **under-discount leverage and dilution**: roughly **$5.6 billion** of equity issuance is planned, and **$47.5 billion** of net debt (~**$88** per share) creates refinancing sensitivity as rates stay elevated. **[Fact]** FY2025 investing cash outflows were **$11.9 billion** while operating cash flow was **$6.9 billion**, so reported free cash flow before dividends was deeply negative during the growth capex cycle. **[Opinion]** There is no obvious dated mispricing edge at today's price; the stock looks fairly valued for a quality regulated compounder, which is why Marvin lands below our **~15%** return hurdle.
